subject: Mathematics
q: The remainder when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>19</mn><mn>200</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>23</mn><mn>200</mn></msup></math> is divided by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn></math>, is _____ .
question: The remainder when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>19</mn><mn>200</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mn>23</mn><mn>200</mn></msup></math> is divided by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn></math>, is _____ .
solution:
Solving 19200+23200=21-2200+21+2200

=2C020021200×20+C220021198×22....C200200210×2200

Now we know that 212=441 is divisible by 49,

So rest of the terms will be divisible by 49 

So lets consider for 2C200200210×2200=2201

Now rewriting the term 2201=2367=7+167

Now 1+767=C067×1+C1677+C26772.........

=1+67×7+49k

Now when dividing 1+767 by 49 we consider 1+67×7 as 49k is divisible by 49

Now  1+67×7=470 which when divided by 49 leaves remainder as 29.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the ratio of the fifth term from the beginning to the fifth term from the end in the expansion of 24+134n is 6:1, then the third term from the beginning is:
question: If the ratio of the fifth term from the beginning to the fifth term from the end in the expansion of 24+134n is 6:1, then the third term from the beginning is:
solution:
Given expansion is24+134n

We know that general term of an expansion x+an is Tr+1=Crnxn-rar

(5th term from beginning)

T4+1=C4n(24)n-4·1344

(5th term from end)

We know that rth term from the end in a binomial expansion is Tn-r+2 from the beginning.

T5'=Tn-4+1=C4n134n-4(24)4

It is also given that the ratio of the fifth term from the beginning to the fifth term from the end is 6:1.

Now, T5T5'=61

⇒2n-84·3n-84=6

∴(6)n-82=(6)

∴n=10

∴T3=C2102481342=45×43

=603

Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the coefficient of xr in the expansion of x+3n−1+x+3n−2x+2+x+3n−3x+22+....+x+2n−1 be αr. If∑r=0nαr=βn−γn,β,γ∈N, then the value of β2+γ2 equals _______.
question: Let the coefficient of xr in the expansion of x+3n−1+x+3n−2x+2+x+3n−3x+22+....+x+2n−1 be αr. If∑r=0nαr=βn−γn,β,γ∈N, then the value of β2+γ2 equals _______.
solution:
Let,

S=x+3n−1+x+3n−2x+2+x+3n−3x+22+.....+x+2n−1

Now, using the sum of G.P formula we get,

⇒S=x+3n-11-x+2x+3n1-x+2x+3

⇒S=x+3n-x+2n

Now, finding the sum of coefficients we get,

⇒∑αr=∑x+3n-∑x+2n

⇒4n−3n=βn−γn by taking x=1

So, on comparing both side we get, β=4, γ=3

Hence, β2+γ2=16+9=25

subject: Mathematics
q: If $S_n=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{1}{{ }^n C_r}$ and $t_n=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{r}{{ }^n C_r}$, then $\frac{t_n}{S_n}$ is equal to
question: If $S_n=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{1}{{ }^n C_r}$ and $t_n=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{r}{{ }^n C_r}$, then $\frac{t_n}{S_n}$ is equal to
solution:
$t=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{r}{{ }^n C_r}=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{n-r}{{ }^n C_{n-r}}=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{n-r}{{ }^n C_r}\left(\because{ }^n C_r={ }^n C_{n-r}\right)$
$2 t_n=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{r+n-r}{{ }^n C_r}=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{n}{{ }^n C_r} \Rightarrow t_n=\frac{n}{2} \sum_{r=0}^n \frac{1}{{ }^n C_r}=\frac{n}{2} S_n \Rightarrow \frac{t_n}{S_n}=\frac{n}{2}$

q: Let for the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>9</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> term in the binomial expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>n</mi></msup></math>, in the increasing powers of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, to be the greatest for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, the least value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is the ratio of the coefficient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>6</mn></msup></math> to the coefficient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is equal to
question: Let for the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>9</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> term in the binomial expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>n</mi></msup></math>, in the increasing powers of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, to be the greatest for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, the least value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is the ratio of the coefficient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>6</mn></msup></math> to the coefficient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>n</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is equal to
solution:
Given, 

3+6xn=3n1+2xn

Now if T9 is numerically greatest term, then T8≤T9≥T10

So, C7n3n-76x7≤C8n3n-86x8≥C9n3n-96x9

⇒  n!n-7!7!9≤n!n-8!8!3·6x≥n!n-9!9!6x2

⇒9n-7n-8⏟≤1832n-88≥369.894⏟

⇒72≤27n-7 and 27≥9n-8

⇒293≤n and n≤11

So, n0=10

For 3+6x10

Now Tr+1=Cr10310-r6xr

For coefficient of x6

r=6⇒C61034·66

For coefficient of x3

r=3⇒C31037·63

So, k=C610C310·34·6637·63=10!7!3!6!4!10!·8

⇒k=14

∴ k+n0=24

subject: Mathematics
q: If the 7th term in the binomial expansion of $\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt[3]{84}}+\sqrt{3} \ln x\right)^9, x>0$, is equal to 729 , then $x$ can be:
question: If the 7th term in the binomial expansion of $\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt[3]{84}}+\sqrt{3} \ln x\right)^9, x>0$, is equal to 729 , then $x$ can be:
solution:
Let $r+1=7 \Rightarrow r=6$
Given expansion is
$$
\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt[3]{84}}+\sqrt{3} \ln x\right)^9, x>0
$$
We have
$$
\mathrm{T}_{r+1}={ }^n \mathrm{C}_r(x)^{n-r} a^r \text { for }(x+a)^n \text {. }
$$
$\therefore$ According to the question
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 729={ }^9 \mathrm{C}_6\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt[3]{84}}\right)^3 \cdot(\sqrt{3} \ln x)^6 \\
& \Rightarrow 3^6=84 \times \frac{3^3}{84} \times 3^3 \times(6 \ln x) \\
& \Rightarrow(\ln x)^6=1 \Rightarrow(\ln x)^6=(\ln e)^6 \\
& \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{e}
\end{aligned}
$$

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> denote greatest integer less than or equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>. If for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>,</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>=</mo><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>j</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>n</mi></mrow></munderover><msub><mi>a</mi><mi>j</mi></msub><msup><mi>x</mi><mi>j</mi></msup></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>j</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>n</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></munderover><msub><mi>a</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>j</mi></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>j</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></munderover><msub><mi>a</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>j</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is equal to :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> denote greatest integer less than or equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>. If for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>,</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>=</mo><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>j</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>n</mi></mrow></munderover><msub><mi>a</mi><mi>j</mi></msub><msup><mi>x</mi><mi>j</mi></msup></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>j</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>n</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></munderover><msub><mi>a</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>j</mi></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>j</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></munderover><msub><mi>a</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>j</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is equal to :
solution:
1-x+x3n=∑j=03najxj

⇒1-x+x3n=a0+a1x+a2x2……+a3nx3n   ...i

Then,

∑j=03n2a2j=a0+a2+a4…… 

∑j=03n-12a2j+1=a1+a3+a5……

Put x=1 in i, then we get

1=a0+a1+a2+a3………+a3n   ...(A)

Put x=-1 in i, then we get

1=a0-a1+a2-a3………+-13na3n    ...(B)

Solving (A) and (B)

a0+a2+a4…..=1

a1+a3+a5……=0

Hence,

∑j=03n2a2j+4∑j=03n-12a2j+1=1

q: If some three consecutive coefficients in the binomial expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>n</mi></msup></math> in powers of <math><mi>x</mi></math> are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>70</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the average of these three coefficients is:
question: If some three consecutive coefficients in the binomial expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>n</mi></msup></math> in powers of <math><mi>x</mi></math> are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>70</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the average of these three coefficients is:
solution:
From the given condition

 nCr:nCr+1:nCr+2=2:15:70

⇒ nCr nCr+1=215 and  nCr+1 nCr+2=1570

⇒ n!n-r!·r!n!n-r-1!·r+1!=215 and  n!n-r-1!·r+1!n!n-r-2!·r+2!=1570

⇒n-r-1!·r+1!n-r!·r!=215 and n-r-2!·r+2!n-r-1!·r+1!=314

⇒n-r-1!·r+1·r!n-r·n-r-1!·r!=215 and n-r-2!·r+2·r+1!n-r-1·n-r-2!·r+1!=314

⇒ r+1n-r=215 and  r+2n-r-1=314

⇒ 17r=2n-15 and 17r=3n-31

⇒3n-31=2n-15, ⇒n=16 and r=1

Hence, average = nCr+nCr+1+nCr+23

= 16C1+16C2+16C33

=232.

subject: Mathematics
q: If $f(y)=1-(y-1)+(y-1)^2-(y-1)^3$ $+\ldots-(y-1)^{17}$ then the coefficient of $y^2$ in it is
question: If $f(y)=1-(y-1)+(y-1)^2-(y-1)^3$ $+\ldots-(y-1)^{17}$ then the coefficient of $y^2$ in it is
solution:
Given function is
$$
\begin{array}{r}
f(y)=1-(y-1)+(y-1)^2-(y-1)^3 \\
+\ldots \ldots \ldots-(y-1)^{17}
\end{array}
$$
In the expansion of $(y-1)^n$
$$
T_{r+1}={ }^n \mathrm{C}_r y^{n-r}(-1)^r
$$
coeff of $y^2$ in $(y-1)^2={ }^2 \mathrm{C}_0$
coeff of $y^2$ in $(y-1)^3={ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1$
coeff of $y^2$ in $(y-1)^4={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2$
So, coeff of termwise is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& { }^2 \mathrm{C}_0+{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3+\ldots \ldots \ldots+{ }^{17} \mathrm{C}_{15} \\
& =1+{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3+\ldots \ldots \ldots . .+{ }^{17} \mathrm{C}_{15} \\
& =\left({ }^3 \mathrm{C}_0+{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1\right)+{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3+\ldots \ldots \ldots . .+{ }^{17} \mathrm{C}_{15} \\
& ={ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_2+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3+\ldots \ldots \ldots+{ }^{17} \mathrm{C}_{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& ={ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2+{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3+\ldots \ldots \ldots+{ }^{17} \mathrm{C}_{15} \\
& ={ }^{18} \mathrm{C}_{15}={ }^{18} \mathrm{C}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q:
The constant term in the expansion of
2x+1x7+3x25 is _____ .
question:
The constant term in the expansion of
2x+1x7+3x25 is _____ .
solution:
Any term in the expansion of 2x+1x7+3x25 is given by

5!a!·b!·c!2xax-7b3x2c

 where a+b+c=5   ...i

=2a3c5!a!·b!·c!xax-7bx2c

=2a3c5!a!·b!·c!xa-7b+2c

Now for term to be independent of x, we know

a-7b+2c=0   ...ii

Solving i & ii, we get

b=c+58

Also, a, b, c∈1,2,3,4,5

So, a=1, b=1, c=3

Therefore, the term independent of x will be 

2133·5!1!·1!·3!=1080

q: Remainder when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>64</mn><msup><mn>32</mn><mn>32</mn></msup></msup></math> is divided by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> is equal to _____.
question: Remainder when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>64</mn><msup><mn>32</mn><mn>32</mn></msup></msup></math> is divided by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> is equal to _____.
solution:
Given,

643232=63+132×32×32....32times

⇒643232=63+132λ

Now we know that by binomial theorem,

1+6332λ=C032λ·1+C132λ·1·63+C232λ·1·632+ .......

⇒1+6332λ=1+9α

So, when 643232 is divided by 9 we get 1 as remainder.

q: The coefficient of <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>9</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>8</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></msup></math>, is
question: The coefficient of <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>9</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>8</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></msup></math>, is
solution:
As the given series is in G.P., so its sum is

1+x101-x1+x111-x1+x

1+x101+x11-x111+x11×11+x=1+x11-x11

Coefficient of x7, is 11C7=11C4=330

q: Let he sum of the coefficient of first three terms in the expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>376</mn></math>. Then, the coefficient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>4</mn></msup></math> is equal to:
question: Let he sum of the coefficient of first three terms in the expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mi>n</mi></msup><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>376</mn></math>. Then, the coefficient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>4</mn></msup></math> is equal to:
solution:
We know that general term in the expansion of x-3x2n is given by,

Tr+1=Crnxn-r-3x2r

=-1r×Crn3r xn-r-2r

Tr+1=-1r×Crn3r xn-3r...............1

So, T1=T0+1= C0n30 xn=xn

T2=-1× C1n31 xn-3

T3=C2n32 xn-6

Now given sum is

1- C1n·3+ C2n·32=376

⇒1-3n+nn-12·9=376

⇒1-3n+n2-n2·9=376

⇒2-6n+9n2-9n=752

⇒9n2-15n-750=0

⇒3n2-5n-250=0

⇒n=5±25+30006

⇒n=5±556

⇒n=10 ignoring negative sign

Now, Tr+1=-1r Cr10 3r x10-3r

So, for coefficient of x4 we take 

10-3r=4

⇒3r=6

⇒r=2

So, coefficient of x4 is given by,

T2+1=-12·C210 32=45×9=405.

q: The term independent of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> in the expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is equal to

=x1/3-1x1/210

q: If the fourth term in the Binomial expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mn>8</mn></msub><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>8</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then a value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: If the fourth term in the Binomial expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mn>8</mn></msub><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>8</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then a value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
The general term in the binomial expansion a+bn is Tr+1=Crnan-rbr.

Given, the fourth term in the expansion of 2x+xlog8x6 is 

T4=20×87

⇒C362x3xlog8x3=20×87

⇒6!3!·3!2x3xlog8x3=20×237

⇒6×5×4×3!3×2×1·3!2x3xlog8x3=20×221

⇒202x3xlog8x3=20×221

⇒2xxlog8x3=273

⇒2xxlog8x=27

⇒1xxlog8x=26

⇒xlog8x-1=82

Taking log both side to the base 8

⇒log8xlog8x-1=log882

Using logamn=nlogam, we get

⇒(log8x-1)log8x=2

⇒t-1t=2                (Let log8x=t)

⇒t2-t-2=0

⇒t=2 or -1

⇒log8x=2 or -1

If logax=b, ⇒x=ab,

⇒x=82 or 8-1

⇒x=64  or 18.

solution:
1-2x+4y2n

We know, if having three variables then total number of terms would be n+1n+22

⇒n+1n+22=28

∴   n+1n+2 =56

Which gives n=6 , and sum of coefficients would be 

1-2+46=36=729 .

solution:
Tr+1=Cr10αx1910-rβx-16r

Tr+1=Cr10α10-rβrx10-r9-r6

Term independent of x ⇒Power of x=0

10-r9-r6=0⇒  r=4

T5=C410α6β4

Now Let α3,β2 are two numbers. Clearly both are positive.

A.M.≥G.M.

⇒  α3+β22≥α3β21/2

⇒  α3β2≤4

⇒  α6β4≤16

⇒  T510C4≤16

⇒  T5≤16.10C4

⇒  T5max=16×10C4=10k

⇒10k=3360

⇒  k=336

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>10</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>9</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>K</mi><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>10</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></math>, then the remainder when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is divided by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> is
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>10</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>9</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>K</mi><mrow><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><msup><mn>3</mn><mn>10</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></math>, then the remainder when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is divided by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> is
solution:
We know xn-yn=x-yxn-1+xn-2y+⋯xyn-2+yn-1

12·310+122·39+⋯·129·32+1210·3

=29+28·3+  ⋯  2·38+39210·316=310-210210·310

So, K=310-210
Since we need the remainder when K is divided by 6

so, 310=6q1+3 and 210=6q2+4
Now K will be of the form 6q1+3-6q2+4
=6q1-q2-1
Hence, when K is divided by 6, we get the remainder as 6-1=5

subject: Mathematics
q: For an integer $\mathrm{n} \geq 2$, if the arithmetic mean of all coefficients in the binomial expansion of $(x+y)^{2 n-3}$ is 16 , then the distance of the point $P\left(2 n-1, n^2-4 n\right)$ from the line $x+y=8$ is:
question: For an integer $\mathrm{n} \geq 2$, if the arithmetic mean of all coefficients in the binomial expansion of $(x+y)^{2 n-3}$ is 16 , then the distance of the point $P\left(2 n-1, n^2-4 n\right)$ from the line $x+y=8$ is:
solution:
No. of terms in $(x+y)^{(2 n-3)} \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{c}(2 n-3+1) \\ (2 n-2)\end{array}\right].$ $\therefore$ sum of all coefficients $=2^{2 n-3}$ (Put $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{y}=1$ ) $\therefore$ Arithmetic mean of all coefficients $\begin{aligned} & =\left(\frac{2^{2 n-3}}{2 n-2}\right)=16 \\ & \Rightarrow 2^{2 n-3}=2^5(n-1) \Rightarrow n=5 \\ & \therefore P\left(2 n-1, n^2-4 n\right)=(9,5) \end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & x+y=8 \\ & \therefore P M=\left|\frac{9+5-8}{\sqrt{2}}\right|=\frac{6}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{3 \times 2}{\sqrt{2}}=3 \sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: In the expansion of $\left(\sqrt[3]{2}+\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{3}}\right)^n, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{N}$, if the ratio of $15^{\text {at }}$ term from the beginning to the $15^{\text {th }}$ term from the end is $\frac{1}{6}$, then the value of ${ }^n C_3$ is:
question: In the expansion of $\left(\sqrt[3]{2}+\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{3}}\right)^n, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{N}$, if the ratio of $15^{\text {at }}$ term from the beginning to the $15^{\text {th }}$ term from the end is $\frac{1}{6}$, then the value of ${ }^n C_3$ is:
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}+1}={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}\left(2^{1 / 3}\right)^{\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{r}}\left(\frac{1}{3^{1 / 3}}\right)^{\mathrm{r}} \\
& \mathrm{r}=14 \\
& \mathrm{~T}_{15}={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{14}\left(2^{1 / 3}\right)^{\mathrm{n}-14}\left(\frac{1}{3^{1 / 3}}\right)^{14}
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{T}_{15}^{\prime}=15^{\mathrm{h}}$ term from last is $(\mathrm{n}-13)^{\mathrm{h}}$ term from beginning.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}_{15}^{\prime}={ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}-14}\left(2^{1 / 3}\right)^{14}\left(\frac{1}{3^{1 / 3}}\right)^{\mathrm{n}-14} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~T}_{15}}{\mathrm{~T}_{15}^{\prime}}=\frac{{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{14}\left(2^{1 / 3}\right)^{\mathrm{n}-14}\left(\frac{1}{3^{1 / 3}}\right)^{14}}{{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}-14}\left(2^{1 / 3}\right)^{14}\left(\frac{1}{3^{1 / 3}}\right)^{\mathrm{n}-14}}=\frac{1}{6}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left(2^{1 / 3}\right)^{n-28}\left(3^{1 / 3}\right)^{n-28}=\frac{1}{6} \\ & =6^{\frac{n-28}{3}}=6^{-1}\end{aligned}$
$\text { So, }{ }^n \mathrm{C}_3={ }^{25} \mathrm{C}_3=2300$

subject: Mathematics
q: The coefficient of <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the binomial expansion of <math><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup></math> where <math><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0,1</mn></math> is
question: The coefficient of <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the binomial expansion of <math><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup></math> where <math><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0,1</mn></math> is
solution:
x13+1 x23-x13+1x23-x13+1-x-1 x+1x x-110

=x13+1-1-1x1210 =x13-x-1210

tr+1=10Crx1310-rx−12r ( −1 ) r

tr+1=10Crx103-5r6-1r

∵ 103-5r6=-5

5r6=506  ⇒r=10

Hence, 10C10-110=1
